does: This paper identifies and characterizes shallow safety alignment as a critical vulnerability in large language models, where safety constraints primarily affect only the earliest output tokens rather than the entire generation process. Through case studies and empirical analysis, the authors demonstrate that current aligned LLMs exhibit this shallow alignment behavior and show how this phenomenon explains multiple known attack vectors including adversarial suffix attacks, prefilling attacks, decoding parameter attacks, and fine-tuning attacks. The core contribution is providing a unified theoretical framework that consolidates seemingly disparate vulnerabilities under a single underlying mechanism of insufficient depth in safety constraints.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research documenting vulnerabilities in aligned language models, extending beyond individual attack demonstrations to propose a unifying principle. It connects to prior work on jailbreaking techniques, adversarial attacks on neural networks, and the broader challenge of robust alignment in deep learning systems. The paper responds to recent discoveries showing that fine-tuning and simple prompting techniques can circumvent safety guardrails, and it engages with mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to understand how alignment guarantees are actually implemented in model weights and activations across the generation trajectory.
enables: This framework opens new research directions for improving alignment robustness by shifting focus from surface-level token distribution modifications to deeper, more persistent safety constraints throughout generation. The shallow alignment concept enables development of diagnostic tools to measure alignment depth and vulnerability, and motivates architectural or training approaches that maintain safety guarantees across all output positions. It provides a concrete research agenda for studying whether existing alignment techniques can be modified to maintain safety properties beyond initial tokens, and how interpretability work might reveal and strengthen these deeper safety mechanisms, ultimately enabling more robust defenses against both current and future jailbreak techniques.
